1→# Literature Review: Finding Non-Compositional Phrases via Future Lens
     2→
     3→## Research Area Overview
     4→
     5→This research sits at the intersection of three areas: (1) mechanistic interpretability of LLMs, specifically how hidden states encode multi-token information; (2) memorization in language models; and (3) non-compositional/idiomatic language processing. The hypothesis is that the Future Lens method can serve as a lower bound detector for phrases that LLMs have memorized as non-compositional units, since predictability of future tokens from a single hidden state indicates the model has encoded the phrase as a single unit rather than composing it from parts.
     6→
     7→## Key Papers
     8→
     9→### 1. Future Lens (Pal et al., 2023) — Core Method
    10→
    11→**Key Contribution**: Demonstrates that individual hidden states in GPT-J-6B encode information about tokens 2+ positions ahead, not just the immediate next token.
    12→
    13→**Methodology**:
    14→- Four methods tested: (a) Direct vocabulary prediction via linear model, (b) Linear model approximation (predict future hidden state, decode with pretrained head), (c) Fixed prompt causal intervention (transplant hidden state into unrelated context), (d) Learned prompt causal intervention (optimize soft prompt to extract future token info).
    15→- Learned prompt method is best: 48.4% accuracy at N=1 (one token ahead of next), peaking at middle layers (~layer 14 of 28 in GPT-J).
    16→- Bigram baseline: 20.1% accuracy at N=1.
    17→
    18→**Key Results**:
    19→- Future token prediction peaks at **middle layers**, unlike N=0 which peaks at last layer.
    20→- Accuracy correlates with model confidence: 95% accuracy when model confidence is 90-100%.
    21→- Named entities show similar (not higher) accuracy (44%, 42%, 37% for N=1,2,3), suggesting future info is encoded broadly.
    22→- Optimizing for N=1 generalizes well to N=2,3; optimizing for other N does not generalize back to N=1.
    23→
    24→**Relevance**: If a hidden state at token t can predict tokens t+2, t+3, etc., this indicates the model has "pre-computed" the entire phrase as a unit. For non-compositional phrases (idioms, named entities), this pre-computation is expected because the model cannot derive the continuation compositionally.
    25→
    26→**Datasets Used**: The Pile (100K training tokens, 1K test tokens).
    27→**Code**: https://github.com/KoyenaPal/future-lens
    28→
    29→### 2. Token Erasure (Feucht et al., 2024) — Complementary Method
    30→
    31→**Key Contribution**: Discovers that last token positions of multi-token words and named entities exhibit "erasure" — information about previous tokens is rapidly forgotten in early layers. Uses this to build an implicit vocabulary reader.
    32→
    33→**Methodology**:
    34→- Train linear probes at each layer to predict nearby tokens (offsets -3 to +1).
    35→- Test on COUNTERFACT subjects: last tokens of multi-token entities show dramatic drop in ability to predict preceding tokens, suggesting the model overwrites token-level info with entity-level semantics.
    36→- Same pattern found for multi-token words in Wikipedia.
    37→- Propose erasure score ψ that measures probability drop between layer 1 and layer 9 for predicting within-sequence tokens.
    38→- Algorithm 1 segments documents into high-scoring non-overlapping lexical items.
    39→
    40→**Key Results**:
    41→- Llama-2-7b: ~1800 sequences recovered from 500 Wikipedia articles; 44.9% are multi-token words or entities.
    42→- Llama-3-8b (larger vocab): fewer multi-token words recovered, but finds multi-word expressions, LaTeX commands.
    43→- Highest-scoring sequences are plausible non-compositional lexical items.
    44→
    45→**Relevance**: The erasure score provides an alternative/complementary signal to Future Lens for detecting non-compositional phrases. Where Future Lens detects "forward prediction" (can predict tokens ahead), Token Erasure detects "backward forgetting" (forgets constituent tokens). Both indicate the model treats the phrase as a single unit.
    46→
    47→**Datasets Used**: The Pile, COUNTERFACT, Wikipedia.
    48→**Code**: https://github.com/sfeucht/footprints
    49→
    50→### 3. Transformer Memorization via Idioms (Haviv et al., 2022)
    51→
    52→**Key Contribution**: First framework for probing memorization recall in transformers using idioms. Discovers two-phase memorization: early-layer candidate promotion + late-layer confidence boosting.
    53→
    54→**Methodology**:
    55→- Define criteria for memorization detection: (1) single target independent of context, (2) irreducible prompt (need entire sequence).
    56→- Construct IdioMem dataset: 852 English idioms with metadata.
    57→- Split idioms into memorized/non-memorized sets per model.
    58→- Track predicted token rank and probability across layers using logit lens interpretation.
    59→
    60→**Key Results**:
    61→- Memorized idioms: target token promoted to top of distribution by early layers (candidate promotion), then probability sharply increases in final layers (confidence boosting).
    62→- Non-memorized inputs: gradual, less structured prediction construction.
    63→- Zeroing out FFN neurons in early layers disrupts memorization recall; late-layer intervention doesn't.
    64→- Pattern holds across GPT-2 sizes and GPT-Neo architectures.
    65→
    66→**Relevance**: Provides ground-truth non-compositional phrases (idioms) for validation. The two-phase memorization profile could serve as a detection signal complementary to Future Lens predictions.
    67→
    68→**Datasets Used**: IdioMem (852 idioms), LAMA-UHN (factual statements).
    69→**Code**: https://github.com/adihaviv/idiomem
    70→
    71→### 4. MWE Semantics in Transformers Survey (Miletic & Schulte im Walde, 2024)
    72→
    73→**Key Finding**: Transformers capture MWE semantics inconsistently, relying on surface patterns and memorized information. MWE meaning is predominantly localized in early layers. Representations benefit from lower semantic idiosyncrasy and ambiguity.
    74→
    75→**Relevance**: Confirms that non-compositional processing happens in early layers, consistent with both Future Lens (middle layer peak) and Token Erasure (early layer erasure).
    76→
    77→### 5. Frequent Phrases vs. Idioms (Rambelli et al., 2023)
    78→
    79→**Key Finding**: Idioms and high-frequency compositional phrases are processed similarly by both humans and neural language models (measured via surprisal). This suggests frequency-based retrieval may confound non-compositionality detection.
    80→
    81→**Relevance**: Important caveat — Future Lens predictions may capture both non-compositional phrases AND highly frequent compositional phrases. Need to distinguish between these cases, possibly using compositionality annotations as ground truth.
    82→
    83→## Common Methodologies
    84→
    85→1. **Logit Lens / Tuned Lens**: Interpreting hidden states as probability distributions over vocabulary by applying the pretrained decoder head. Used in Haviv et al. and related to Future Lens linear methods.
    86→2. **Causal Intervention**: Transplanting hidden states between contexts to test what information they encode (Future Lens, ROME).
    87→3. **Linear Probing**: Training linear classifiers on hidden states to test what information is accessible (Token Erasure).
    88→4. **Erasure/Knockout**: Zeroing or modifying specific components to test necessity (Haviv et al. FFN knockouts).
    89→
    90→## Standard Baselines
    91→
    92→- **Bigram frequency**: Simple co-occurrence statistics (Future Lens reports 20.1% bigram baseline at N=1).
    93→- **Logit Lens (N=0)**: Standard next-token prediction from hidden states.
    94→- **Random baseline**: Chance-level prediction (~0.002% for 50K vocabulary).
    95→
    96→## Evaluation Metrics
    97→
    98→- **Precision@k**: Whether the correct token appears in top-k predictions.
    99→- **Surprisal**: Negative log probability of predicted token under the model.
   100→- **Erasure score ψ**: Drop in probe accuracy between early and middle layers (Token Erasure).
   101→- **Compositionality correlation**: Agreement with human compositionality ratings.
   102→
   103→## Datasets in the Literature
   104→
   105→| Dataset | Papers | Task |
   106→|---------|--------|------|
   107→| The Pile | Future Lens, Token Erasure | General text, training data |
   108→| COUNTERFACT | Token Erasure | Named entity factual recall |
   109→| IdioMem | Haviv et al. | 852 English idioms for memorization probing |
   110→| LAMA-UHN | Haviv et al. | Factual statements |
   111→| Farahmand NCS | (external) | 1042 noun compounds with compositionality labels |
   112→| MAGPIE | (external) | 44K sentences with idiom annotations |
   113→| Reddy et al. | (external) | 90 noun compounds with compositionality scores |
   114→
   115→## Gaps and Opportunities
   116→
   117→1. **No direct application of Future Lens to non-compositionality detection**: The original paper mentions named entities but does not systematically evaluate on known non-compositional phrases.
   118→2. **Future Lens + Token Erasure combination**: Both methods detect related signals (forward prediction vs. backward erasure) but have not been combined.
   119→3. **Compositionality scoring**: Future Lens accuracy or confidence could serve as a continuous compositionality score, which can be validated against human annotations.
   120→4. **Scale beyond GPT-J**: Future Lens was only tested on GPT-J-6B; Token Erasure used Llama models. Testing on modern models would be valuable.
   121→5. **Distinguishing frequency from non-compositionality**: Rambelli et al. show both are processed similarly; need a method to tease them apart.
   122→
   123→## Recommendations for Experiment
   124→
   125→### Recommended Approach
   126→1. **Apply Future Lens to known non-compositional phrases** (IdioMem idioms, Farahmand non-compositional compounds) and measure whether prediction accuracy is higher than for compositional phrases.
   127→2. **Compare Future Lens predictions with Token Erasure scores** on the same phrases to see if the signals correlate.
   128→3. **Use compositionality-annotated datasets** (Farahmand, Reddy) as ground truth to validate that Future Lens accuracy correlates with non-compositionality.
   129→
   130→### Recommended Datasets
   131→- **Primary**: IdioMem (ground-truth idioms), Farahmand NCS (compositionality labels)
   132→- **Secondary**: COUNTERFACT (named entities), Wikipedia text (general)
   133→- **Baseline text**: Pile samples (compositional text)
   134→
   135→### Recommended Baselines
   136→- Bigram/n-gram frequency statistics
   137→- Standard logit lens (N=0 only)
   138→- Random/uniform baseline
   139→
   140→### Recommended Metrics
   141→- Future Lens accuracy (Precision@1) at N=1, N=2, N=3 for non-compositional vs. compositional phrases
   142→- Correlation between Future Lens confidence and human compositionality ratings
   143→- Token Erasure score comparison between phrase types
   144→- ROC/AUC for binary non-compositionality classification using Future Lens as a feature
   145→
   146→### Methodological Considerations
   147→- Use smaller models (GPT-2, Pythia) for feasibility without GPU cluster
   148→- The learned prompt method requires per-layer training; linear methods are cheaper
   149→- Ensure phrases appear in training data to distinguish non-compositionality from unfamiliarity
   150→- Control for phrase frequency to separate memorization-by-frequency from memorization-by-non-compositionality
   151→

file.content:
# Literature Review: Finding Non-Compositional Phrases via Future Lens

## Research Area Overview

This research sits at the intersection of three areas: (1) mechanistic interpretability of LLMs, specifically how hidden states encode multi-token information; (2) memorization in language models; and (3) non-compositional/idiomatic language processing. The hypothesis is that the Future Lens method can serve as a lower bound detector for phrases that LLMs have memorized as non-compositional units, since predictability of future tokens from a single hidden state indicates the model has encoded the phrase as a single unit rather than composing it from parts.

## Key Papers

### 1. Future Lens (Pal et al., 2023) — Core Method

**Key Contribution**: Demonstrates that individual hidden states in GPT-J-6B encode information about tokens 2+ positions ahead, not just the immediate next token.

**Methodology**:
- Four methods tested: (a) Direct vocabulary prediction via linear model, (b) Linear model approximation (predict future hidden state, decode with pretrained head), (c) Fixed prompt causal intervention (transplant hidden state into unrelated context), (d) Learned prompt causal intervention (optimize soft prompt to extract future token info).
- Learned prompt method is best: 48.4% accuracy at N=1 (one token ahead of next), peaking at middle layers (~layer 14 of 28 in GPT-J).
- Bigram baseline: 20.1% accuracy at N=1.

**Key Results**:
- Future token prediction peaks at **middle layers**, unlike N=0 which peaks at last layer.
- Accuracy correlates with model confidence: 95% accuracy when model confidence is 90-100%.
- Named entities show similar (not higher) accuracy (44%, 42%, 37% for N=1,2,3), suggesting future info is encoded broadly.
- Optimizing for N=1 generalizes well to N=2,3; optimizing for other N does not generalize back to N=1.

**Relevance**: If a hidden state at token t can predict tokens t+2, t+3, etc., this indicates the model has "pre-computed" the entire phrase as a unit. For non-compositional phrases (idioms, named entities), this pre-computation is expected because the model cannot derive the continuation compositionally.

**Datasets Used**: The Pile (100K training tokens, 1K test tokens).
**Code**: https://github.com/KoyenaPal/future-lens

### 2. Token Erasure (Feucht et al., 2024) — Complementary Method

**Key Contribution**: Discovers that last token positions of multi-token words and named entities exhibit "erasure" — information about previous tokens is rapidly forgotten in early layers. Uses this to build an implicit vocabulary reader.

**Methodology**:
- Train linear probes at each layer to predict nearby tokens (offsets -3 to +1).
- Test on COUNTERFACT subjects: last tokens of multi-token entities show dramatic drop in ability to predict preceding tokens, suggesting the model overwrites token-level info with entity-level semantics.
- Same pattern found for multi-token words in Wikipedia.
- Propose erasure score ψ that measures probability drop between layer 1 and layer 9 for predicting within-sequence tokens.
- Algorithm 1 segments documents into high-scoring non-overlapping lexical items.

**Key Results**:
- Llama-2-7b: ~1800 sequences recovered from 500 Wikipedia articles; 44.9% are multi-token words or entities.
- Llama-3-8b (larger vocab): fewer multi-token words recovered, but finds multi-word expressions, LaTeX commands.
- Highest-scoring sequences are plausible non-compositional lexical items.

**Relevance**: The erasure score provides an alternative/complementary signal to Future Lens for detecting non-compositional phrases. Where Future Lens detects "forward prediction" (can predict tokens ahead), Token Erasure detects "backward forgetting" (forgets constituent tokens). Both indicate the model treats the phrase as a single unit.

**Datasets Used**: The Pile, COUNTERFACT, Wikipedia.
**Code**: https://github.com/sfeucht/footprints

### 3. Transformer Memorization via Idioms (Haviv et al., 2022)

**Key Contribution**: First framework for probing memorization recall in transformers using idioms. Discovers two-phase memorization: early-layer candidate promotion + late-layer confidence boosting.

**Methodology**:
- Define criteria for memorization detection: (1) single target independent of context, (2) irreducible prompt (need entire sequence).
- Construct IdioMem dataset: 852 English idioms with metadata.
- Split idioms into memorized/non-memorized sets per model.
- Track predicted token rank and probability across layers using logit lens interpretation.

**Key Results**:
- Memorized idioms: target token promoted to top of distribution by early layers (candidate promotion), then probability sharply increases in final layers (confidence boosting).
- Non-memorized inputs: gradual, less structured prediction construction.
- Zeroing out FFN neurons in early layers disrupts memorization recall; late-layer intervention doesn't.
- Pattern holds across GPT-2 sizes and GPT-Neo architectures.

**Relevance**: Provides ground-truth non-compositional phrases (idioms) for validation. The two-phase memorization profile could serve as a detection signal complementary to Future Lens predictions.

**Datasets Used**: IdioMem (852 idioms), LAMA-UHN (factual statements).
**Code**: https://github.com/adihaviv/idiomem

### 4. MWE Semantics in Transformers Survey (Miletic & Schulte im Walde, 2024)

**Key Finding**: Transformers capture MWE semantics inconsistently, relying on surface patterns and memorized information. MWE meaning is predominantly localized in early layers. Representations benefit from lower semantic idiosyncrasy and ambiguity.

**Relevance**: Confirms that non-compositional processing happens in early layers, consistent with both Future Lens (middle layer peak) and Token Erasure (early layer erasure).

### 5. Frequent Phrases vs. Idioms (Rambelli et al., 2023)

**Key Finding**: Idioms and high-frequency compositional phrases are processed similarly by both humans and neural language models (measured via surprisal). This suggests frequency-based retrieval may confound non-compositionality detection.

**Relevance**: Important caveat — Future Lens predictions may capture both non-compositional phrases AND highly frequent compositional phrases. Need to distinguish between these cases, possibly using compositionality annotations as ground truth.

## Common Methodologies

1. **Logit Lens / Tuned Lens**: Interpreting hidden states as probability distributions over vocabulary by applying the pretrained decoder head. Used in Haviv et al. and related to Future Lens linear methods.
2. **Causal Intervention**: Transplanting hidden states between contexts to test what information they encode (Future Lens, ROME).
3. **Linear Probing**: Training linear classifiers on hidden states to test what information is accessible (Token Erasure).
4. **Erasure/Knockout**: Zeroing or modifying specific components to test necessity (Haviv et al. FFN knockouts).

## Standard Baselines

- **Bigram frequency**: Simple co-occurrence statistics (Future Lens reports 20.1% bigram baseline at N=1).
- **Logit Lens (N=0)**: Standard next-token prediction from hidden states.
- **Random baseline**: Chance-level prediction (~0.002% for 50K vocabulary).

## Evaluation Metrics

- **Precision@k**: Whether the correct token appears in top-k predictions.
- **Surprisal**: Negative log probability of predicted token under the model.
- **Erasure score ψ**: Drop in probe accuracy between early and middle layers (Token Erasure).
- **Compositionality correlation**: Agreement with human compositionality ratings.

## Datasets in the Literature

| Dataset | Papers | Task |
|---------|--------|------|
| The Pile | Future Lens, Token Erasure | General text, training data |
| COUNTERFACT | Token Erasure | Named entity factual recall |
| IdioMem | Haviv et al. | 852 English idioms for memorization probing |
| LAMA-UHN | Haviv et al. | Factual statements |
| Farahmand NCS | (external) | 1042 noun compounds with compositionality labels |
| MAGPIE | (external) | 44K sentences with idiom annotations |
| Reddy et al. | (external) | 90 noun compounds with compositionality scores |

## Gaps and Opportunities

1. **No direct application of Future Lens to non-compositionality detection**: The original paper mentions named entities but does not systematically evaluate on known non-compositional phrases.
2. **Future Lens + Token Erasure combination**: Both methods detect related signals (forward prediction vs. backward erasure) but have not been combined.
3. **Compositionality scoring**: Future Lens accuracy or confidence could serve as a continuous compositionality score, which can be validated against human annotations.
4. **Scale beyond GPT-J**: Future Lens was only tested on GPT-J-6B; Token Erasure used Llama models. Testing on modern models would be valuable.
5. **Distinguishing frequency from non-compositionality**: Rambelli et al. show both are processed similarly; need a method to tease them apart.

## Recommendations for Experiment

### Recommended Approach
1. **Apply Future Lens to known non-compositional phrases** (IdioMem idioms, Farahmand non-compositional compounds) and measure whether prediction accuracy is higher than for compositional phrases.
2. **Compare Future Lens predictions with Token Erasure scores** on the same phrases to see if the signals correlate.
3. **Use compositionality-annotated datasets** (Farahmand, Reddy) as ground truth to validate that Future Lens accuracy correlates with non-compositionality.

### Recommended Datasets
- **Primary**: IdioMem (ground-truth idioms), Farahmand NCS (compositionality labels)
- **Secondary**: COUNTERFACT (named entities), Wikipedia text (general)
- **Baseline text**: Pile samples (compositional text)

### Recommended Baselines
- Bigram/n-gram frequency statistics
- Standard logit lens (N=0 only)
- Random/uniform baseline

### Recommended Metrics
- Future Lens accuracy (Precision@1) at N=1, N=2, N=3 for non-compositional vs. compositional phrases
- Correlation between Future Lens confidence and human compositionality ratings
- Token Erasure score comparison between phrase types
- ROC/AUC for binary non-compositionality classification using Future Lens as a feature

### Methodological Considerations
- Use smaller models (GPT-2, Pythia) for feasibility without GPU cluster
- The learned prompt method requires per-layer training; linear methods are cheaper
- Ensure phrases appear in training data to distinguish non-compositionality from unfamiliarity
- Control for phrase frequency to separate memorization-by-frequency from memorization-by-non-compositionality

1→# Resources Catalog
     2→
     3→## Summary
     4→This document catalogs all resources gathered for the research project on finding non-compositional phrases using the Future Lens method. The hypothesis is that Future Lens can lower-bound the set of phrases LLMs have memorized as non-compositional units, since predictability in advance from a hidden state indicates likely memorization as a single unit.
     5→
     6→## Papers
     7→Total papers downloaded: 12
     8→
     9→| Title | Authors | Year | File | Key Info |
    10→|-------|---------|------|------|----------|
    11→| Future Lens | Pal, Sun, Yuan, Wallace, Bau | 2023 | papers/2311.04897_future_lens.pdf | Core method: predict future tokens from hidden states |
    12→| Token Erasure | Feucht, Atkinson, Wallace, Bau | 2024 | papers/2406.20086_token_erasure.pdf | Erasure score for implicit vocabulary items |
    13→| Transformer Memorization via Idioms | Haviv et al. | 2022 | papers/2210.03588_transformer_memorization_idioms.pdf | IdioMem dataset, two-phase memorization |
    14→| Memorization Without Overfitting | Tirumala et al. | 2022 | papers/2310.04691_memorization_without_overfitting.pdf | Training dynamics of memorization |
    15→| SoK: Memorization in LLMs | Hartmann et al. | 2023 | papers/2310.09638_sok_memorization_llms.pdf | Taxonomy of memorization types |
    16→| MWE Semantics in Transformers | Miletic & Schulte im Walde | 2024 | papers/2401.12299_mwe_transformer_survey.pdf | Survey: MWE processing localized in early layers |
    17→| Figurative Language in Narratives | Chakrabarty et al. | 2021 | papers/2109.03400_figurative_language_narratives.pdf | Non-compositional language interpretation |
    18→| FF Layers as Key-Value Memories | Geva et al. | 2020 | papers/2012.04905_ff_layers_key_value_memories.pdf | Foundational: how transformers store knowledge |
    19→| Frequent Phrases vs Idioms | Rambelli et al. | 2023 | papers/2309.13415_frequent_phrases_idioms.pdf | Frequency vs non-compositionality in processing |
    20→| Memorization or Reasoning (Idioms) | Kim et al. | 2025 | papers/2501.00794_memorization_or_reasoning_idioms.pdf | MIDAS dataset, hybrid processing |
    21→| Non-comp Expression Generation | Zhou et al. | 2023 | papers/2310.10013_non_comp_expression_generation.pdf | Curriculum learning for non-comp generation |
    22→| Demystifying Verbatim Memorization | Huang et al. | 2024 | papers/2407.17817_demystifying_verbatim_memorization.pdf | Memorization intertwined with general capabilities |
    23→
    24→See papers/README.md for detailed descriptions.
    25→
    26→## Datasets
    27→Total datasets downloaded: 6
    28→
    29→| Name | Source | Size | Task | Location | Notes |
    30→|------|--------|------|------|----------|-------|
    31→| IdioMem | GitHub (adihaviv) | 852 idioms | Memorization probing | datasets/idiomem.jsonl | Ground-truth non-compositional phrases |
    32→| CounterFact Expanded | GitHub (sfeucht) | 54K prompts | Entity knowledge | datasets/counterfact_expanded.csv | Named entity factual recall |
    33→| Wikipedia Test 500 | GitHub (sfeucht) | 500 articles | General text probing | datasets/wikipedia_test_500.csv | Multi-token word analysis |
    34→| Future Lens Test Data | GitHub (KoyenaPal) | 1K samples | Future token prediction | datasets/testing_data_teacher_1000.csv | Pile samples with predictions |
    35→| Farahmand NCS | GitHub (meghdadFar) | 1042 compounds | Compositionality labels | datasets/farahmand_ncs/ | Expert binary annotations |
    36→| IdioTS | HuggingFace | 195 examples | Idiom comprehension | datasets/idiots/ | English + Spanish idioms |
    37→
    38→See datasets/README.md for detailed descriptions and download instructions.
    39→
    40→## Code Repositories
    41→Total repositories cloned: 3
    42→
    43→| Name | URL | Purpose | Location | Notes |
    44→|------|-----|---------|----------|-------|
    45→| future-lens | github.com/KoyenaPal/future-lens | Core Future Lens implementation | code/future-lens/ | GPT-J-6B, uses nnsight |
    46→| footprints | github.com/sfeucht/footprints | Token erasure & implicit vocabulary | code/footprints/ | Llama models, pre-trained probes on HF |
    47→| idiomem | github.com/adihaviv/idiomem | Idiom memorization experiments | code/idiomem/ | 852 idioms dataset + analysis |
    48→
    49→See code/README.md for detailed descriptions.
    50→
    51→## Resource Gathering Notes
    52→
    53→### Search Strategy
    54→- Used paper-finder service (diligent mode) with query: "Future Lens non-compositional phrases language model memorization"
    55→- 44 papers returned, filtered to 12 most relevant
    56→- Focused on: (1) the Future Lens method itself, (2) token erasure/implicit vocabulary, (3) idiom/MWE processing in transformers, (4) memorization mechanisms in LLMs
    57→- Searched for compositionality-annotated datasets via web search
    58→
    59→### Selection Criteria
    60→- Papers directly related to the core method (Future Lens, Token Erasure) were prioritized
    61→- Papers providing ground-truth datasets for non-compositional phrases (idioms, MWEs) were included
    62→- Papers on general memorization included for theoretical grounding
    63→- Only papers with available code/data preferred for reproducibility
    64→
    65→### Challenges Encountered
    66→- MAGPIE HuggingFace dataset uses deprecated dataset scripts; could not be auto-downloaded
    67→- No single dataset combines compositionality scores with the specific format needed for Future Lens evaluation
    68→- Future Lens was only tested on GPT-J-6B; applying to other models requires adaptation
    69→
    70→### Gaps and Workarounds
    71→- **Missing**: A ready-made dataset of phrases with both compositionality scores AND appearance in LLM training data. Workaround: use IdioMem (known non-compositional) + Farahmand NCS (compositionality labels) + general text (compositional baseline).
    72→- **Missing**: Pre-trained Future Lens probes for models other than GPT-J. Workaround: use linear methods (cheaper to train) or adapt existing footprints probes.
    73→
    74→## Recommendations for Experiment Design
    75→
    76→### Primary Experiment: Future Lens as Non-Compositionality Detector
    77→1. **Primary datasets**: IdioMem (idioms = non-compositional) + Farahmand NCS (labeled compositionality) + Wikipedia text (compositional baseline)
    78→2. **Baseline methods**: Bigram frequency, logit lens (N=0), random baseline
    79→3. **Evaluation metrics**: Precision@1 for Future Lens predictions on non-comp vs. comp phrases; correlation with compositionality ratings; ROC/AUC for binary classification
    80→4. **Code to adapt/reuse**:
    81→   - `code/future-lens/` for linear model approximation and causal intervention
    82→   - `code/footprints/` for erasure score computation (complementary signal)
    83→   - `code/idiomem/` for ground-truth idioms and memorization analysis framework
    84→
    85→### Recommended Model
    86→- Start with GPT-2 or Pythia (smaller, faster iteration)
    87→- Future Lens linear methods are cheapest to train
    88→- Token Erasure probes are available pre-trained for Llama-2-7b on HuggingFace
    89→
    90→### Key Research Questions
    91→1. Does Future Lens prediction accuracy correlate with non-compositionality?
    92→2. Can erasure score + Future Lens accuracy together improve non-compositionality detection?
    93→3. What is the precision/recall tradeoff when using Future Lens as a binary non-compositionality classifier?
    94→

# Resources Catalog

## Summary
This document catalogs all resources gathered for the research project on finding non-compositional phrases using the Future Lens method. The hypothesis is that Future Lens can lower-bound the set of phrases LLMs have memorized as non-compositional units, since predictability in advance from a hidden state indicates likely memorization as a single unit.

## Papers
Total papers downloaded: 12

| Title | Authors | Year | File | Key Info |
|-------|---------|------|------|----------|
| Future Lens | Pal, Sun, Yuan, Wallace, Bau | 2023 | papers/2311.04897_future_lens.pdf | Core method: predict future tokens from hidden states |
| Token Erasure | Feucht, Atkinson, Wallace, Bau | 2024 | papers/2406.20086_token_erasure.pdf | Erasure score for implicit vocabulary items |
| Transformer Memorization via Idioms | Haviv et al. | 2022 | papers/2210.03588_transformer_memorization_idioms.pdf | IdioMem dataset, two-phase memorization |
| Memorization Without Overfitting | Tirumala et al. | 2022 | papers/2310.04691_memorization_without_overfitting.pdf | Training dynamics of memorization |
| SoK: Memorization in LLMs | Hartmann et al. | 2023 | papers/2310.09638_sok_memorization_llms.pdf | Taxonomy of memorization types |
| MWE Semantics in Transformers | Miletic & Schulte im Walde | 2024 | papers/2401.12299_mwe_transformer_survey.pdf | Survey: MWE processing localized in early layers |
| Figurative Language in Narratives | Chakrabarty et al. | 2021 | papers/2109.03400_figurative_language_narratives.pdf | Non-compositional language interpretation |
| FF Layers as Key-Value Memories | Geva et al. | 2020 | papers/2012.04905_ff_layers_key_value_memories.pdf | Foundational: how transformers store knowledge |
| Frequent Phrases vs Idioms | Rambelli et al. | 2023 | papers/2309.13415_frequent_phrases_idioms.pdf | Frequency vs non-compositionality in processing |
| Memorization or Reasoning (Idioms) | Kim et al. | 2025 | papers/2501.00794_memorization_or_reasoning_idioms.pdf | MIDAS dataset, hybrid processing |
| Non-comp Expression Generation | Zhou et al. | 2023 | papers/2310.10013_non_comp_expression_generation.pdf | Curriculum learning for non-comp generation |
| Demystifying Verbatim Memorization | Huang et al. | 2024 | papers/2407.17817_demystifying_verbatim_memorization.pdf | Memorization intertwined with general capabilities |

See papers/README.md for detailed descriptions.

## Datasets
Total datasets downloaded: 6

| Name | Source | Size | Task | Location | Notes |
|------|--------|------|------|----------|-------|
| IdioMem | GitHub (adihaviv) | 852 idioms | Memorization probing | datasets/idiomem.jsonl | Ground-truth non-compositional phrases |
| CounterFact Expanded | GitHub (sfeucht) | 54K prompts | Entity knowledge | datasets/counterfact_expanded.csv | Named entity factual recall |
| Wikipedia Test 500 | GitHub (sfeucht) | 500 articles | General text probing | datasets/wikipedia_test_500.csv | Multi-token word analysis |
| Future Lens Test Data | GitHub (KoyenaPal) | 1K samples | Future token prediction | datasets/testing_data_teacher_1000.csv | Pile samples with predictions |
| Farahmand NCS | GitHub (meghdadFar) | 1042 compounds | Compositionality labels | datasets/farahmand_ncs/ | Expert binary annotations |
| IdioTS | HuggingFace | 195 examples | Idiom comprehension | datasets/idiots/ | English + Spanish idioms |

See datasets/README.md for detailed descriptions and download instructions.

## Code Repositories
Total repositories cloned: 3

| Name | URL | Purpose | Location | Notes |
|------|-----|---------|----------|-------|
| future-lens | github.com/KoyenaPal/future-lens | Core Future Lens implementation | code/future-lens/ | GPT-J-6B, uses nnsight |
| footprints | github.com/sfeucht/footprints | Token erasure & implicit vocabulary | code/footprints/ | Llama models, pre-trained probes on HF |
| idiomem | github.com/adihaviv/idiomem | Idiom memorization experiments | code/idiomem/ | 852 idioms dataset + analysis |

See code/README.md for detailed descriptions.

## Resource Gathering Notes

### Search Strategy
- Used paper-finder service (diligent mode) with query: "Future Lens non-compositional phrases language model memorization"
- 44 papers returned, filtered to 12 most relevant
- Focused on: (1) the Future Lens method itself, (2) token erasure/implicit vocabulary, (3) idiom/MWE processing in transformers, (4) memorization mechanisms in LLMs
- Searched for compositionality-annotated datasets via web search

### Selection Criteria
- Papers directly related to the core method (Future Lens, Token Erasure) were prioritized
- Papers providing ground-truth datasets for non-compositional phrases (idioms, MWEs) were included
- Papers on general memorization included for theoretical grounding
- Only papers with available code/data preferred for reproducibility

### Challenges Encountered
- MAGPIE HuggingFace dataset uses deprecated dataset scripts; could not be auto-downloaded
- No single dataset combines compositionality scores with the specific format needed for Future Lens evaluation
- Future Lens was only tested on GPT-J-6B; applying to other models requires adaptation

### Gaps and Workarounds
- **Missing**: A ready-made dataset of phrases with both compositionality scores AND appearance in LLM training data. Workaround: use IdioMem (known non-compositional) + Farahmand NCS (compositionality labels) + general text (compositional baseline).
- **Missing**: Pre-trained Future Lens probes for models other than GPT-J. Workaround: use linear methods (cheaper to train) or adapt existing footprints probes.

## Recommendations for Experiment Design

### Primary Experiment: Future Lens as Non-Compositionality Detector
1. **Primary datasets**: IdioMem (idioms = non-compositional) + Farahmand NCS (labeled compositionality) + Wikipedia text (compositional baseline)
2. **Baseline methods**: Bigram frequency, logit lens (N=0), random baseline
3. **Evaluation metrics**: Precision@1 for Future Lens predictions on non-comp vs. comp phrases; correlation with compositionality ratings; ROC/AUC for binary classification
4. **Code to adapt/reuse**:
   - `code/future-lens/` for linear model approximation and causal intervention
   - `code/footprints/` for erasure score computation (complementary signal)
   - `code/idiomem/` for ground-truth idioms and memorization analysis framework

### Recommended Model
- Start with GPT-2 or Pythia (smaller, faster iteration)
- Future Lens linear methods are cheapest to train
- Token Erasure probes are available pre-trained for Llama-2-7b on HuggingFace

### Key Research Questions
1. Does Future Lens prediction accuracy correlate with non-compositionality?
2. Can erasure score + Future Lens accuracy together improve non-compositionality detection?
3. What is the precision/recall tradeoff when using Future Lens as a binary non-compositionality classifier?

Excellent! I have:
- 4x NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPUs (49GB each) - plenty of GPU power
- .venv already exists
- pyproject.toml already exists
- Rich set of papers, datasets, and code repos

The research is about using Future Lens to detect non-compositional phrases. Let me check the existing code and datasets more carefully, then plan and execute.

Key insight from literature review:
- Future Lens predicts future tokens from hidden states (middle layers)
- If a hidden state can predict tokens 2+ positions ahead, the model likely memorized the phrase as a unit
- We can validate this on known non-compositional phrases (idioms from IdioMem, compounds from Farahmand NCS)


- Now I need to examine the available code and datasets to see what I can leverage, then set up a comparison of prediction accuracy between non-compositional and compositional phrases.

content.content:
1→# Future-Lens
     2→Code and Data Repo for the CoNLL 2023 Paper -- Future Lens: Anticipating Subsequent Tokens from a Single Hidden State
     3→
     4→![alt text](img/main.png "Main Fig")
     5→
     6→In this repo, we include two distinctive way to reveal the extent to which individual hidden states may directly encode subsequent tokens: 1) **Linear Model Approximation** and 2) **Causal Intervention Methods**. With the best result, we propose **Future Lens** as the tool to extract information about future (beyond subsequent) tokens from a single hidden token representation.
     7→
     8→## Set-up
     9→Run the following code to install relevant packages to your virtual environment
    10→```
    11→pip install -r scripts/colab-reqs/future-env.txt
    12→```
    13→## Linear Model Approximation
    14→To run the linear modoel approximation, you may run the following command:
    15→```
    16→python linear_methods/linear_hs.py
    17→```
    18→
    19→## Causal Method
    20→To train a soft prompt for GPT-J, you may run the following command:
    21→```
    22→python causal_methods/train.py
    23→```
    24→And then test by running the following script:
    25→```
    26→python causal_methods/test.py
    27→```
    28→Note: To load the default training set, you can access the file with [this link](https://baulab.us/u/koyena/data/future-lens/training_data_teacher_100000.csv).
    29→
    30→## Future Lens
    31→We provide an online [demo](https://colab.research.google.com/github/KoyenaPal/future-lens/blob/main/demo/FutureLensDemonstration.ipynb) notebook for the Future Lens. You may also run the code locally from 
    32→```
    33→demo/FutureLensDemonstration.ipynb
    34→```
    35→## Updates
    36→
    37→### Oct.24th 2025
    38→Updated Future Lens Demo folder with the latest NNsight version (0.5.9).
    39→
    40→### Oct.6th 2023
    41→Our paper is accepted by CoNLL 2023!
    42→
    43→### Nov.8th 2023
    44→We release the code and preprint version of the paper! 
    45→
    46→
    47→## Contact
    48→If any questions about the code, please contact Koyena at pal.k@northeastern.edu.
    49→

    10→import torch, baukit

    12→import pandas as pd

    28→def get_json_input(input_json_file):
    29→    f = open(input_json_file)
    30→    data = json.load(f)
    31→    input_lists = []
    32→    for i in data:
    33→        input_lists.append(i)
    34→    f.close()
    35→    return input_lists
    36→

    38→def get_robust_test_json(input_json_file):
    39→    f = open(input_json_file)
    40→    data = json.load(f)
    41→    f.close()
    42→    return data
    43→
    44→
    45→def get_linear_model_test_input_original_results(full_pile_sample_file, robust_results_dir, token_pos=1, layer=26):
    46→    full_pile_sample = get_robust_test_json(full_pile_sample_file)
    47→    input_lists = []
    48→    for curr_ind in range(len(full_pile_sample)):
    49→        robust_result_json_file = robust_results_dir + "/" + \
    50→        f"robustTest_InputID_{curr_ind}_from_pile_all_layers_[{layer}].json"
    51→        curr_robust_dict = get_robust_test_json(robust_result_json_file)
    52→        flattened_list_of_score_list = [item for sublist in curr_robust_dict['scores'] for item in sublist]
    53→        result = [sum(items) for items in zip(*flattened_list_of_score_list)]
    54→        if (result[token_pos] > 2):
    55→            curr_sample = full_pile_sample[curr_ind]
    56→            input_lists.append(curr_sample)
    57→    return input_lists
    58→
    59→
    60→def get_training_testing_data(input_json_file, testing_size=0.4, seed=51):
    61→    random.seed(seed)
    62→    input_lists = get_json_input(input_json_file)
    63→    total_test_size = int(testing_size * len(input_lists))
    64→    testing_indices = random.sample(range(len(input_lists)), total_test_size)
    65→    training_data = []
    66→    testing_data = []
    67→    for curr_ind in range(len(input_lists)):
    68→        if curr_ind in testing_indices:
    69→            testing_data.append(input_lists[curr_ind])
    70→        else:
    71→            training_data.append(input_lists[curr_ind])
    72→    training_data = pd.DataFrame(training_data)
    73→    testing_data = pd.DataFrame(testing_data)
    74→    return training_data, testing_data
    75→
    76→def get_sub_hs(token_indices, ref_hs, layers=list(range(28))):
    77→    """
    78→    Function that gets the hidden state values of the token indices to use for the edit hs function in TraceDict. 
    79→    
    80→    Parameters:
    81→        token_indices: token index values of hidden states we should take
    82→        ref_hs: referenced hidden states that we will copy from
    83→        layers: the set of layers we want to copy. 
    84→    
    85→    Returns:
    86→        Replacement HS: replacement hidden states
    87→    """
    88→    saved_hs = []
    89→    for layer in layers:
    90→        layer_hs = []
    91→        for token_index in token_indices:
    92→            layer_hs.append(ref_hs[layer, 0, token_index])
    93→        saved_hs.append(layer_hs)
    94→    return saved_hs
    95→
    96→def get_hidden_states(model, tok, prefix, device="cuda:0"):
    97→    inp = {k: torch.tensor(v)[None].to(device) for k, v in tok(prefix).items()}
    98→    layer_names = [n for n, _ in model.named_modules()
    99→                    if re.match(r'^transformer.wte|^transformer.h.\d+$', n)]
   100→    with TraceDict(model, layer_names) as tr:
   101→        logits = model(**inp)['logits']
   102→    return torch.stack([tr[ln].output[0][None,:] if ln=="transformer.wte" else tr[ln].output[0] for ln in layer_names])
   103→
   104→def create_input_output_pair(model, tok, doc, input_config, output_config, device):
   105→    """
   106→    input_config = [token position, layer] ex. [10,transformer.h.26]
   107→    output_config = [token position, layer] ex. [11,transformer.h.26]
   108→    """   
   109→    hs = get_hidden_states(model, tok, doc, device)
   110→    source_hs = get_sub_hs(input_config[0], hs, layers=input_config[1])
   111→    target_hs = get_sub_hs(output_config[0], hs, layers=output_config[1])
   112→    return source_hs[0][0], target_hs[0][0]
   113→
   114→class HiddenStateDataset(Dataset):
   115→    def __init__(self, input_configs, output_configs, texts, device):
   116→        self.input_configs = input_configs
   117→        self.output_configs = output_configs
   118→        self.texts = texts
   119→        self.device = device
   120→
   121→    def __len__(self):
   122→        return len(self.texts)
   123→
   124→    def __getitem__(self, idx):
   125→        total_text = self.texts[idx]
   126→        curr_in_config = self.input_configs[idx]
   127→        curr_out_config = self.output_configs[idx]
   128→        source_hs, target_hs = create_input_output_pair(TEACHER_MODEL,
   129→                                                        TEACHER_TOK,
   130→                                                        total_text,
   131→                                                        curr_in_config,
   132→                                                        curr_out_config, self.device)
   133→        total_text_tokens = TEACHER_TOK(total_text)['input_ids']
   134→        wanted_token = total_text_tokens[curr_out_config[0][0]+1]
   135→        sample = (source_hs, target_hs, wanted_token)
   136→        return sample
   137→    
   138→class HiddenStateDatasetBatch(Dataset):
   139→    def __init__(self, input_configs, output_configs, texts, device, npy_path, next_token_skip):
   140→        self.input_configs = input_configs
   141→        self.output_configs = output_configs
   142→        self.texts = texts
   143→        self.device = device
   144→        self.npy_path = npy_path
   145→        self.next_token_skip = next_token_skip
   146→
   147→    def __len__(self):
   148→        return len(self.texts)
   149→
   150→    def __getitem__(self, idx):
   151→        if self.npy_path is None:
   152→            return (self.input_configs[idx], self.output_configs[idx], self.texts[idx], 
   153→                    self.device, None, self.next_token_skip)
   154→        else:
   155→            return (self.input_configs[idx], self.output_configs[idx], self.texts[idx], 
   156→                    self.device, self.npy_path[idx], self.next_token_skip)
   157→    
   158→    
   159→def dataset_collate_func(batch):
   160→    source_hs_list = []
   161→    target_hs_list = []
   162→    wanted_token_list = []
   163→    for idx in range(len(batch)):
   164→        curr_in_config = batch[idx][0]
   165→        curr_out_config = batch[idx][1]
   166→        total_text = batch[idx][2]
   167→        device = batch[idx][3]
   168→        # should be a list later
   169→        npy_path = batch[idx][4]
   170→        next_token_skip = batch[idx][5]
   171→        source_hs = None
   172→        target_hs = None
   173→        if npy_path is not None and os.path.exists(npy_path[0]):
   174→            source_path = npy_path[0]
   175→            target_path = npy_path[1]
   176→            source_hs = torch.tensor(np.load(source_path)[curr_in_config[1]]).squeeze().cuda()
   177→            target_hs = torch.tensor(np.load(target_path)[next_token_skip + 1]).cuda()
   178→        else:
   179→            source_hs, target_hs = create_input_output_pair(TEACHER_MODEL,
   180→                                                            TEACHER_TOK,
   181→                                                            total_text,
   182→                                                            curr_in_config,
   183→                                                            curr_out_config, device)
   184→        
   185→        total_text_tokens = TEACHER_TOK(total_text)['input_ids']
   186→        wanted_token = total_text_tokens[curr_out_config[0][0]+1]
   187→        source_hs_list.append(source_hs)
   188→        target_hs_list.append(target_hs)
   189→        wanted_token_list.append(torch.tensor(wanted_token))
   190→    source_hs_list = torch.stack(source_hs_list)
   191→    target_hs_list = torch.stack(target_hs_list)
   192→    wanted_token_list = torch.stack(wanted_token_list)
   193→    return (source_hs_list,target_hs_list, wanted_token_list)
   194→    
   195→def create_dataset(tok, data, next_token_skip=0, in_layer=26, out_layer=27, device='cuda:0', npy_paths=None):
   196→    total_texts = []
   197→    input_configs = []
   198→    output_configs = []
   199→    for _, curr_train in data.iterrows():
   200→        total_text = "<|endoftext|>" + str(curr_train['decoded_prefix']) + str(curr_train['decoded_phrase'])
   201→        total_text_tokens = tok(total_text)['input_ids']
   202→        if len(total_text_tokens) > 2048:
   203→            total_text = tok.decode(total_text_tokens[-2048:])
   204→        actual_gen_len = len(tok(str(curr_train['decoded_phrase']))['input_ids'])
   205→        # including +1s to the layer bc -1 is now considered as embedding layer
   206→        input_config = [[-actual_gen_len - 1], [in_layer + 1]]
   207→        output_config = [[-actual_gen_len + next_token_skip], [out_layer + 1]]          
   208→        total_texts.append(total_text)
   209→        input_configs.append(input_config)
   210→        output_configs.append(output_config)
   211→    dataset_obj = HiddenStateDatasetBatch(input_configs, output_configs, total_texts, 
   212→                                          device, npy_paths, next_token_skip)
   213→    return dataset_obj
   214→
   215→def get_test_token_output(hs):
   216→    probs = torch.nn.functional.softmax(DECODER(hs), dim=-1)
   217→    _, favorite_tokens = probs.topk(k=1, dim=-1)
   218→    return favorite_tokens 
   219→
   220→
   221→def criterion(test_hs, expected_hs, T=1):
   222→
   223→    p_s = F.log_softmax(DECODER(test_hs)/T, dim=-1)
   224→    p_t = F.softmax(DECODER(expected_hs)/T, dim=-1)
   225→    loss = (F.kl_div(p_s, p_t,reduction='batchmean')* (T**2))
   226→    # so that the model doesn't aim for it to go to negatives
   227→    total_loss = loss
   228→    return total_loss
   229→
   230→# Define a function to calculate the accuracy
   231→def calculate_accuracy(outputs, targets, wanted_tokens):
   232→    gt_correct = 0
   233→    correct = 0
   234→    wanted_token = wanted_tokens.item()
   235→    pred = get_test_token_output(outputs).item()
   236→    actual = get_test_token_output(targets).item()
   237→    if pred == actual:
   238→        correct += 1
   239→    if pred == wanted_token:
   240→        gt_correct += 1
   241→    return correct, gt_correct
   242→
   243→class LinearModel(nn.Module):
   244→    def __init__(self, input_size, output_size):
   245→        super(LinearModel, self).__init__()
   246→        self.fc = nn.Linear(input_size, output_size)
   247→
   248→    def forward(self, x):
   249→        output = self.fc(x)
   250→        return output
   251→    
   252→def train(args, model_s, device, train_loader, test_loader,  optimizer, scheduler, epoch, highest_acc=None, highest_model_save=None):
   253→    model_s.train()
   254→    total_loss = 0.0
   255→    correct = 0
   256→    gt_correct = 0
   257→    total_seen = 0
   258→    optimizer.zero_grad()
   259→    for batch_idx, (data, target, wanted_token) in enumerate(train_loader):
   260→        data, target, wanted_token = data.to(device), target.to(device), wanted_token.to(device)
   261→        total_seen += len(wanted_token)
   262→        output_s = model_s(data).to(device)
   263→        output_t = target
   264→        loss_kd = criterion(output_s, output_t)
   265→        loss = loss_kd
   266→        loss.backward()
   267→        total_loss += loss.item() * data.size(0)
   268→        
   269→        batch_train_seen = 0
   270→        batch_correct = 0
   271→        batch_gt_correct = 0
   272→        if batch_idx % 5 == 0 and batch_idx > 0:
   273→            torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model_s.parameters(), 1.0)
   274→            optimizer.step()
   275→            optimizer.zero_grad()
   276→        for i, output_set in enumerate(output_s):
   277→                curr_correct, curr_gt_correct = calculate_accuracy(output_set, target[i], wanted_token[i])
   278→                correct += curr_correct
   279→                gt_correct += curr_gt_correct
   280→            
   281→                batch_correct += curr_correct
   282→                batch_gt_correct += curr_gt_correct
   283→                batch_train_seen += 1
   284→        # Log metrics to wandb for each GPU set
   285→        if batch_idx % args["log_interval"] == 0:
   286→            print('Train Epoch: {} [{}/{} ({:.0f}%)]\tBatch Loss: {:.6f}'.format(
   287→                epoch, batch_idx * len(data), len(train_loader.dataset),
   288→                100. * batch_idx / len(train_loader), loss.item()))
   289→            if "log_file" in args:
   290→                log_file = args["log_file"]
   291→                with open(log_file, 'a') as file:
   292→                    file.write('Train Epoch: {} [{}/{} ({:.0f}%)]\tBatch Loss: {:.6f}\n'.format(
   293→                        epoch, batch_idx * len(data), len(train_loader.dataset),
   294→                        100. * batch_idx / len(train_loader), loss.item()))
   295→                    file.flush()
   296→            args["test_results"] = os.path.join(args["experiment_data_dir"],
   297→                                                f"linear_model_{display_name}_epoch_{epoch}_batch_idx_{batch_idx}_results.csv")
   298→
   299→            test_loss, acc, gt_acc = test(args, model_s, device, test_loader)
   300→            model_save_path = os.path.join(args["experiment_data_dir"],
   301→                       f'model_trained_{display_name}_epoch_{epoch}_batch_idx_{batch_idx}.pth')
   302→            if (highest_acc is None) or (highest_acc < acc):
   303→                highest_acc = acc
   304→                highest_model_save = model_save_path
   305→
   306→            wandb.log({"test/loss": test_loss, "test/accuracy": acc,
   307→                       "test/groundtruth_accuracy": gt_acc,
   308→                       "test/train-epoch": epoch, "test/train-batches-seen": batch_idx + epoch*batch_idx,
   309→                      "test/highest-acc": highest_acc, "test/highest_model_save": str(highest_model_save)})
   310→            wandb.log({"train/loss": loss.item(), "train/accuracy": 100 * batch_correct / batch_train_seen,
   311→                        "train/groundtruth_accuracy": 100 * batch_gt_correct / batch_train_seen,
   312→                        "train/train-epoch": epoch, "train/train-batches-seen": batch_idx + epoch*batch_idx})
   313→            torch.save(model_s.state_dict(), model_save_path)
   314→
   315→            model_s.train()

   317→

   323→def train_new(args, model_s, train_loader, device):
   324→    def calculate_linear_regression(X, Y, singular_value_threshold):
   325→        # Calculate the means of X and Y
   326→        X_mean = torch.mean(X, axis=0)
   327→        Y_mean = torch.mean(Y, axis=0)
   328→        # Center X and Y
   329→        X_centered = X - X_mean
   330→        Y_centered = Y - Y_mean        
   331→        # Compute the SVD of centered X
   332→        U, S, Vt = torch.linalg.svd(X_centered, full_matrices=False)
   333→        # Apply the threshold to the singular values
   334→        S_inv = torch.where(S > singular_value_threshold, 1.0/S, 0.0)
   335→        num_fulfilled = torch.sum(S_inv.nonzero()).item()
   336→        # Compute the pseudoinverse of centered X using the SVD
   337→        X_centered_pinv = torch.matmul(Vt.T, torch.matmul(torch.diag(S_inv), U.T))
   338→        # Compute A using least squares
   339→        A = torch.matmul(X_centered_pinv, Y_centered)
   340→        # Compute bias term b
   341→        b = Y_mean - torch.matmul(X_mean, A)
   342→        return A, b, num_fulfilled
   343→
   344→
   345→    # Assuming X and Y are your data
   346→    # my batch size is the full dataset
   347→    for _, (data, target, wanted_token) in enumerate(train_loader):
   348→        data, target, wanted_token = data.to(device), target.to(device), wanted_token.to(device)
   349→        singular_value_threshold = 0.0
   350→        A, b, dim_s_inv = calculate_linear_regression(data, target, singular_value_threshold)
   351→
   352→        with torch.no_grad():
   353→            model_s.fc.weight[...] = A.T
   354→            model_s.fc.bias[...] = b
   355→
   356→        display_name = args["display_name"]
   357→        args["test_results"] = os.path.join(args["experiment_data_dir"],
   358→                               f"linear_model_train_{display_name}_full_math_results.csv")
   359→        
   360→        #train_loss, train_acc, train_gt_acc = test(args, model_s, device, train_loader)
   361→        saved_model_path = os.path.join(args["experiment_data_dir"],f'model_trained_{display_name}_math_sol.pth')
   362→        torch.save(model_s.state_dict(), saved_model_path)
   363→        return
   364→

   366→def test(args, model, device, test_loader):
   367→    model.eval()
   368→    total_loss = 0
   369→    gt_correct = 0
   370→    correct = 0
   371→    results = []
   372→    with torch.no_grad():
   373→        for _, (data, target, wanted_token) in baukit.pbar(enumerate(test_loader)):
   374→            data, target, wanted_token = data.to(device), target.to(device), wanted_token.to(device)
   375→            output_s = model(data).to(device)
   376→
   377→            loss = criterion(output_s, target)
   378→            total_loss += loss.item() * data.size(0)
   379→            for i, output_set in enumerate(output_s):
   380→                pred = get_test_token_output(output_set).item()
   381→                actual = get_test_token_output(target[i]).item()
   382→                curr_result = {"pred_tok": pred, "pred": TEACHER_TOK.decode(pred),
   383→                        "actual_tok": actual, "actual": TEACHER_TOK.decode(actual),
   384→                        "groundtruth_tok": wanted_token[i].item(), "groundtruth": TEACHER_TOK.decode(wanted_token[i])}
   385→                results.append(curr_result)
   386→                if pred == actual:
   387→                    correct += 1
   388→                if pred == wanted_token[i].item():
   389→                    gt_correct += 1
   390→    test_loss = total_loss / len(test_loader.dataset)
   391→    acc = 100 * correct / len(test_loader.dataset)
   392→    gt_acc = 100 * gt_correct / len(test_loader.dataset)
   393→    if "test_results" in args:
   394→        csv_file = args["test_results"]
   395→        result_df = pd.DataFrame(results)
   396→        result_df.to_csv(csv_file)
   397→    print('Total correct: {}\n'.format(correct))
   398→    print('Total groundtruth correct: {}\n'.format(gt_correct))
   399→    print('Test Loss: {}  Accuracy: {}%\n'.format(test_loss, acc))
   400→    print('Groundtruth Accuracy: {}%\n'.format(gt_acc))
   401→    if "log_file" in args:
   402→        log_file = args["log_file"]
   403→        with open(log_file, 'a') as file:
   404→            file.write(f'Testing result at {args["log_file"]}\n')
   405→            file.write('Total correct: {}\n'.format(correct))
   406→            file.write('Total groundtruth correct: {}\n'.format(gt_correct))
   407→            file.write('Test Loss: {}  Accuracy: {}%\n'.format(test_loss, acc))
   408→            file.write('Groundtruth Accuracy: {}%\n'.format(gt_acc))
   409→            file.flush()
   410→    return test_loss, acc, gt_acc

1→import torch
     2→import argparse
     3→import os
     4→from pytorch_lightning import Trainer, seed_everything
     5→from transformers import AutoTokenizer
     6→from customized import CustomizedGPTJForCausalLM
     7→from data import SoftPromptDataModule
     8→from module import GPTJModule
     9→from pytorch_lightning.loggers import TensorBoardLogger
    10→import json
    11→
    12→
    13→def train(args):
    14→    torch_dtypes = {
    15→        "fp32": (torch.float32, 32),
    16→        "fp16": (torch.float16, "16-mixed"),
    17→        "bf16": (torch.bfloat16, "bf16-mixed")
    18→    }
    19→
    20→    if torch.cuda.get_device_name() in ["NVIDIA H100 PCIe", "NVIDIA A100 80GB PCIe"]:
    21→        torch.set_float32_matmul_precision('medium')
    22→
    23→    torch_dtype, training_precision = torch_dtypes[args.precision]
    24→
    25→    if "gpt-j-6b" not in args.model_name_or_path:
    26→        raise ValueError("This script is only for GPT-J-6B.")
    27→    
    28→    logger = TensorBoardLogger(save_dir=args.output_path, name="logs")
    29→    model = CustomizedGPTJForCausalLM.from_pretrained(args.model_name_or_path, torch_dtype=torch_dtype).cuda()
    30→    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(args.model_name_or_path, padding_side="left")
    31→    if tokenizer.pad_token is None:
    32→        tokenizer.add_special_tokens({'pad_token': '<|PAD|>'})
    33→        model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tokenizer))
    34→
    35→    data_module = SoftPromptDataModule(
    36→        model = model,
    37→        tokenizer = tokenizer, 
    38→        train_file_path = args.train_set, 
    39→        training_examples = args.training_examples,
    40→        train_batch_size = args.batch_size, 
    41→        max_length = args.max_length,
    42→        next_token_skip = args.next_token_skip,
    43→        in_layer = args.in_layer,
    44→        out_layer = args.out_layer
    45→    )
    46→
    47→    model = GPTJModule(
    48→        model=model,
    49→        tokenizer=tokenizer, 
    50→        batch_size=args.batch_size,
    51→        prefix_length=args.prefix_length, 
    52→        in_layer = args.in_layer,
    53→        out_layer = args.out_layer,
    54→        next_token_skip = args.next_token_skip,
    55→        lr=args.lr,
    56→        weight_decay=args.weight_decay,
    57→    )
    58→
    59→    trainer = Trainer(
    60→        accelerator="gpu",
    61→        devices=[0],
    62→        max_epochs=args.epochs,
    63→        logger=logger,
    64→        enable_checkpointing=False,
    65→        precision=training_precision,
    66→        accumulate_grad_batches=args.accumulate_grad_batches,
    67→        log_every_n_steps=200
    68→    )
    69→
    70→    logger.log_hyperparams(vars(args))
    71→    trainer.fit(model, train_dataloaders=data_module.train_dataloader())
    72→    model.save(os.path.join(args.output_path, "soft_prefix.pt"))
    73→
    74→    print("Done!")
    75→
    76→
    77→def main():
    78→    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    79→    # Directories
    80→    parser.add_argument('--model_name_or_path', default="EleutherAI/gpt-j-6b")
    81→    parser.add_argument('--output_path', default=None, type=str)
    82→    parser.add_argument('--train_set', default="../data/training_data_teacher_100000.csv")
    83→    parser.add_argument('--training_examples', default=10000, type=int)
    84→
    85→    # Experiment Set-ups
    86→    parser.add_argument('--prefix_length', default=10, type=int, choices=[10, 30])
    87→    parser.add_argument('--next_token_skip', default=[1], type=int, nargs="+") # 0 for the currently predicted token, 1 for the next ...
    88→    parser.add_argument('--in_layer', default=13, type=int)
    89→    parser.add_argument('--out_layer', default=27, type=int)
    90→
    91→    # Hyperparameters
    92→    parser.add_argument('--precision', default="bf16", choices=["fp32", "fp16", "bf16"])
    93→    parser.add_argument('--batch_size', default=2, type=int)
    94→    parser.add_argument('--max_length', default=2048, type=int)
    95→    parser.add_argument('--epochs', default=1, type=int)
    96→    parser.add_argument('--lr', default=5e-4, type=float)
    97→    parser.add_argument('--weight_decay', default=1e-5, type=float)
    98→    parser.add_argument('--accumulate_grad_batches', default=4, type=int)
    99→
   100→    # Seed
   101→    parser.add_argument('--seed', default=42, type=int)
   102→    args = parser.parse_args()
   103→
   104→    if args.output_path is None:
   105→        tk = "".join([str(i) for i in args.next_token_skip])
   106→        args.output_path = "./results/training/layer{}to{}_tk{}".format(args.in_layer, args.out_layer, tk)
   107→
   108→    if not os.path.exists(args.output_path):
   109→        os.makedirs(args.output_path, exist_ok=True)
   110→
   111→    seed_everything(args.seed)
   112→    train(args)
   113→
   114→
   115→if __name__ == "__main__":
   116→    main()
